data.title: Southern Company: Hold Regardless Of Upcoming Earnings, Still A Core Utility Position
data.description:
The Southern Company (SO) is one of the largest multi-utility firms in the US. As with most utilities, SO is currently considered overvalued, and even though it is a core utility holding, investors should watch from the sidelines. SO operates regulated electric and natural gas utilities and a merchant power business. While earnings will be consistently competitive with the sector, the overall unpredictability of the stock markets, interest rates, and geopolitics could override the desire to add to safe and secure income-generating utility investments.

Southern Company Business

Southern Company serves 9 million customers with electric customers in three states (Alabama, Georgia, and Mississippi) and natural gas customers in four states (Illinois, Georgia, Virginia, and Tennessee). From a customer count vantage point, SO is about evenly split between the two, with 4.6 million electric customers and 4.4 million gas customers.

Southern Company's regulated electric business includes Alabama Power, Georgia Power, and Mississippi Power. SO generates 46 gigawatts of regulated power, with the most famous being the recently updated Vogtle nuclear power plant. Southern has a robust capital plan to satisfy a growing demand from organic growth, population and industrial growth, and, of course, data centers. SO Management continues to increase its 5-year capital investment budget, now estimated at $81 billion, after increasing the budget by 28%, or $18 billion, since January 2025. About half the proposed investments will support growing energy demand with new gas-fueled and renewable generation, along with investments in transmission and distribution.

There are no new nuclear facilities in the plan, while there are considerations for additional nuclear power upgrades. Southern Co. has been burned in the past with substantial cost overruns and completion delays. The Vogtle nuclear units #3 and #4 led the way with overruns of $5+ billion and a 5-year delay in operation. SO was also attempting to build the first-ever lignite coal-to-gas power plant in Kemper, MS, which included a coal gasification process combined with carbon capture technology. However, after 3 years behind schedule and a $6 billion pre-tax write-off, management converted the facility into a traditional combined-cycle gas-fired facility.

While these two headline projects rattled investors, overall Southern Co. has moved past these projects with new generating projects slated to be smaller and more manageable. SO has received approval to add 10GW of new capacity, with the latest investment revisions including ~36% from purchased power, ~27% from renewables, ~21% from storage, and 16% from gas-fired facilities.

On the natural gas local distribution LDC side, Southern Company operates major distribution networks with Nicor Gas in Illinois, Atlanta Gas Light, Virginia Natural Gas, and Chattanooga Gas. By comparison, Atmos Energy (ATO) is considered the largest independent LDC, with 3.3 million customers. While the political pressure to replace natural gas usage with electricity has faded into the background for now, the service territory of SO’s LDC is not historically considered as a hotbed of forced conversion activity.

Southern Company’s merchant power business, Southern Power Company, manages unregulated power-generating assets, mainly renewable and natural gas power. Southern Power generates 13+ MW from 5 facilities in 15 states and mostly utilizes long-term supply contracts. While not a large part of SO at less than 10% of revenues, the merchant power business is anchored in 10-year contracts, providing a steady $2.1 billion in annual sales.

Regulatory Outlook

Historically, Southern Company operated in some of the best regulatory environments. My personal archives of S&P’s proprietary State Regulatory Environment date from 2006, with the latest ranking below as of Nov 2025. As shown, Southern Co regulatory environments remain highly rated for support of the utilities under the state’s jurisdiction: SO electric - Alabama, Georgia, and Mississippi are in Above Average categories; SO natural gas—Tennessee is rated Above Average, Virginia is rated the highest level of Average, and Illinois is the lowest level of Average. Overall, investors should seek out utilities in the best regulatory environment and search for service territories in the Above Average/1 to Average/1. Southern Company offers one of the best, most supportive regulatory footprints in the sector.

The Wall Street Journal published an article in Dec 2025 outlining the cost of electricity by state. In the article, WSJ offered the following graphic outlining the inflation-adjusted changes in electric utility rates from 2010 to 2024 for all customer categories. In addition, Visual Capitalist offers a graphic identifying the average cost of electricity for residential and commercial customers by state as of Oct 2025.

As shown by the three graphics above, Southern Co. operates in preferred regulatory environments, has offered historically stable inflation-adjusted electricity pricing, and offers competitive electricity rates in the $0.134 to $0.152 range per kWh. In a politically charged atmosphere with utility pricing becoming a partisan hot potato, Southern Co. is well positioned to ride out the political storm. Recently, Alabama Power and the Alabama Public Service Commission agreed to freeze residential rates for 3 years, and the program was approved by the state legislature with the Governor’s approval on April 2, 2026. Even though Alabama has competitive electricity rates, the rate freeze stalls the political issue until well past the 2028 election cycle.

Earnings & Dividends

Even with Southern Co.'s past of substantial project write-offs and cost overruns of the early 2020s, CFRA rates SO as A- for its Quality Rating. CFRA’s criteria include 10-year consistency in earnings and dividend growth. Using the Schwab stock screener, there are 107 companies listed as ‘regulated electric’ and ‘multi-utility’ peers, of which 24 were rated A- and A, and no peers were rated the highest, A+. Of these 107 peers, only 9 have earned a higher Quality Rating than SO.

Reviewing Seeking Alpha earnings estimates, SO is expected to generate $4.56 per share this year, $5.35 in 2028, and $6.10 in 2030. Management is forecasting 7% to 9% annual EPS growth at least through 2030.

Quarterly earnings are due to be released in the premarket on April 30. Non-GAAP EPS are expected to be $1.21, flat with last year’s $1.23. In Feb, management offered guidance for the 1st quarter at $1.20, below the then-consensus of $1.27.

Southern Co. has paid a dividend since 1947. With the anticipated late April 2026 dividend hike, SO will be elevated to a Dividend Aristocrat, leaping past the threshold of 25 consecutive years of dividend increases. The consensus is for a quarterly increase from $0.74 to $0.76 for a 2.8% hike. This is in line with its 5-year dividend growth rate of 2.94% - but barely keeping up with inflation. The current payout ratio is 73%, a bit higher than management’s goal of a 65% payout ratio. This implies future dividend growth to potentially be below earnings growth until the payout ratio declines to target.

Risks

The two biggest risks for Southern Co. and its investors are short-term interest rate trends and regulatory environment changes.

As with the entire utility sector, share prices are directly reflective of the current yield environment. If interest rates increase, share prices will decrease. The current yield is 3.12% based on a $2.96 annual dividend, which is below current CD and Treasury debt rates at any data point 1 year and longer.

The bigger risk to investors is a change in the regulatory environment of its primary states of Georgia, Alabama, and Mississippi. AL is under a 3-year rate freeze, and GA approved last year both a 5-year power supply plan and a 3-year customer rate plan. Much of SO’s historic success has hinged on its outstanding relationship with both customers and regulators by providing relatively low-cost, reliable utility services. Consistent operating execution should be the foundation of continuing these shareholder-beneficial relationships. It appears that SO management fully understands and appreciates that state regulators are the sole gatekeepers to utility profitability—for the benefit of both the company and its investors.

Southern Co.'s aggressive capital investment plan of $81 billion over the next five years will likely require significant equity and debt issuance. Southern may need to issue $11+ billion of equity, and debt needs could exceed $10 billion a year to fund management's current plan.

Recommendation

Southern Company is a core, long-term, multi-utility holding for both income and utility portfolios. I have owned the stock uninterrupted for well over 15 years, with my initial position taken in the 1980s. The latest purchase was in Feb 2024 at $67, and this review is my 22nd on Seeking Alpha since 2013. The last SA review of SO was on 8/4/2023 with a Buy recommendation, and the stock was valued at $68 a share, for a 4.1% yield. Since that last review, according to Seeking Alpha, SO has rewarded long-term investors with a 51% total return vs. the S&P 500 at 57%. The investment thesis has not changed - Southern Co operates in the best regulatory environments in the country, has positive underlying growth potential, and offers stable income. What has changed is its valuation and the current unpredictability in the economy and financial markets.

Driven by strong demand growth prospects, earnings could expand by 6% to 8% annually, but they are in line with many of its better-quality utility peers. With a higher current payout ratio, dividend growth will be about half of earnings growth, barely keeping up with anticipated inflation.

The current investing environment is extremely unpredictable, with an overabundance of unanswered issues. Will oil settle at $150 or $40? Will inflation continue to be stubbornly above targets? Will US government debt and budget deficits finally take their toll on the US Dollar? The list of potential resolutions to these conditions, and others, makes the current investment environment too unpredictable, except for possibly speculative traders.

For these reasons, Southern Company is recommended as a Hold, awaiting either better future valuations or an easing of the current economic unpredictability.

data.title: Cohen & Steers: Q1 Resilience Holds Fort For Q2 Recovery
data.description:
Cohen & Steers, Inc. (CNS), managed to stage a pretty resilient Q1, impressive given everything that had been happening in the markets, with the worst of the hits to AUM happening towards the very end of Q1. But the recovery in markets that continues today on account of what seems to be a more enduring ceasefire situation, with the military action stopping for the moment on all relevant fronts, and also the opening of the Strait of Hormuz appearing to be taking place, which can help refill terminals and stave off a more acute energy crisis with oil simply not arriving where it is needed, we don't expect much more pain in CNS financials. We are looking at a downward translation in the yield curve, which is a key thing for the mega-cap-skewed markets but as critical for CNS, which has a lean towards high-duration equity hybrids like preference share products and real estate and infrastructure.

This has always been the focus of our coverage. Reinflation concerns really mattered for these asset classes, and that risk is hopefully going to evaporate with the possible end to the supply shock in oil. On the other hand, AM isn't such a high-multiple industry, particularly with the competitive threat of passive management, and forward multiples are close to 20x, which is higher than peers also indexed to a normalization in markets and rate assumptions. On that basis, we're not interested in playing the recovery with CNS, where any quasi-indexing asset management company is just as leveraged to the broad-based recovery that could come from an enduring end to the war.

CNS Results

The company recently disclosed the latest 3-month results, ended March 31st. In terms of AUM by strategy, AUM only shrank YoY for preferred securities products; the overall AUM growth of under 9% reflects the almost same figure in YoY revenue growth. The rate cutting that started in 2024 permitted recovery in CNS's product classes, namely infrastructure, U.S. real estate, and then the global classes of infrastructure and international real estate. Preferreds were the only class that didn't see AUM growth YoY.

The strongest AUM growth was in the international classes, and this reflects CNS' indexation towards inflation expectation dynamics and the yield curve in general. Infrastructure and real estate are both highly sensitive to leverage conditions in terms of private and public market values since the return on these assets is so sensitive to rates on the significant leverage used for these assets. Global cuts began before and went deeper than the U.S. cuts in rates as the U.S. was dealing with a more stubborn inflation; therefore, the stronger AUM recovery than the U.S. asset classes.

While rate cuts had started in 2024 in the U.S. as well, it did spend a while at a stable rate before starting to be brought down on growth concerns, arguably brought on by the low-hire and low-fire environment as a consequence of AI adoption in the white-collar world.

The Iran War brought on reinflation concerns. The yield curve came up quite a bit, though with the weaker USD outlook, now with money retreating from it as a safe haven to pour into risk assets and move away from the falling yields on Treasuries at the moment, we should be seeing a translation downwards of the yield curve. The upward translation is bad for real estate and infrastructure, but it is especially bad for ultra-long-duration bets like preferreds, which fundamentally are like low-seniority, super-long-term debt, with significant sensitivity through duration to long-term structural rates. With greater geopolitical risks and conflict, manifesting in this case in the Strait of Hormuz bottleneck, long-term rates also came up as war risk and geopolitical breakdown raised structural inflation expectations. Preferreds were hit hard and were a depreciating class since the beginning of the year.

But all their U.S. strategies have performed relatively poorly since the beginning of the year because of the U.S. yield curve's sensitivity to the Iran War and energy price level risks. While reinflation was a threat for all markets, the U.S., which had not completely conquered inflation, was more at risk than most of the developed world. Therefore, the global strategies performed relatively better.

Now, with it appearing that the Strait is reopened for commercial traffic thanks to a ceasefire on the fronts of the War, where traffic recently was still very limited during the U.S. blockade of Iranian ports, terminals can restock on crude products, and a supply crunch is no longer as much of a concern. Crude prices have fallen significantly today, and a sustained end to the war will permit the energy markets to normalize, with the futures curve implying as soon as July.

The declines in AUM since last month to March's end, which were considerable at around 5%, driven mostly by declines in value, should be reversing promptly in line with the broader market's total recovery, also driven by assets that are effectively yield sensitive, namely tech through large horizon values and present value sensitivity to discount rates. The sequential pressure, which CNS withstood well based on the flat revenue evolutions March ended, should feel some relief as questions about whether we're going back to a higher-for-longer situation in the U.S. may start to evaporate, supposing, of course, the ceasefire holds.

Bottom Line

The issue is that, looking at Seeking Alpha data, the forward P/E is 19x on this equity. Annualizing EPS from Q1 gets you to around 20x as well. That's high. We like AM at 10x. That is our rule of thumb for the industry, but it is also very possible to find today in European markets, for example, which are also benefiting from a recovery with the Strait opening. While it's true that some of the fundamentals in the underlying markets, namely real estate, have good space for recovery based on metrics like housing starts, European AM companies, which direct significant client funds into U.S. assets necessarily as the U.S. market dominates the world by market cap and most products are quasi-indexed, also stand to benefit significantly from the better geopolitical outlook as of today. CNS stock looks expensive to us.

data.title: lululemon: From Market Darling To Mispriced Turnaround Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I last covered lululemon athletica inc. (LULU), I highlighted how the significant decline in the stock opened a compelling turnaround opportunity despite macro pressure, with significant internal changes underway.

With the stock getting even more attractive amid important macro developments, LULU remains a Buy, with solid potential to recover, especially if a new CEO takes over and successfully takes advantage of the company’s strong foundation with zero debt.

Adapting To The New Reality

Despite beating the market’s top- and bottom-line estimates during their latest quarter, LULU still fell as a result of their disappointing guidance, with a 1% decrease in the Americas (comparable sales; down 2% in constant dollars) and continued strong performance internationally, where they grew by 20% (16% in constant currency) in 2025.

The company’s free cash flow declined significantly, reaching $921.68 million last year compared to $1.58 billion in FY24 and $1.64 billion in FY23, which came despite relatively stable CAPEX, as a result of working capital changes due to tax payables and inventories. The year was marked significantly by a 520 basis point hit in gross margin from the tariffs, despite their efficiency initiatives generating a 110 basis point tailwind, which translated into a $275 million gross tariff cost and $62 million from their mitigation strategies.

For 2026, they expect this to lead to an even deeper hit of $380 million, slightly offset by $160 million in savings, as the new tariffs and the end of the duty exemption loophole (de minimis) add significant pressure for the company in a “new era” of trade, as imports under $800 will no longer enter the US duty- and tax-free. So, even if the tariff situation has relaxed and we don’t see as much news about it anymore, the issues are far from over, and the company may have to rethink its supply chain because of it.

Other than that, LULU expects 2% to 4% growth in net revenue, to $11.35 billion to $11.5 billion, and diluted EPS of $12.1 to $12.3 - without assuming any buybacks - while advancing on their efforts to offset these hits.

Financially, based on LULU’s latest report, we continue to see a very strong position, with the current assets covering the company’s total liabilities with zero debt to worry about and $1.8 billion in cash and cash equivalents available, providing excellent levels of flexibility and supporting the company’s long-term mitigation efforts.

On top of that, lululemon bought back a solid $1.178 billion worth of stock in 2025, representing a solid 6.26% buyback yield based on today's price, further highlighting the company’s capital allocation flexibility.

Macro Uncertainty Fuels Volatility

The latest development regarding the Iran conflict is the recent ceasefire agreement and opening of the Strait of Hormuz, which wiped a big chunk of the current war premiums from key commodities like oil, although it’s important to remember that we’re talking about a conflict that’s already expected to cause significant pressure in the near term regardless of its end date.

As I highlighted in previous articles, even if we see peace in the Middle East overnight, the damage to the region’s energy infrastructure has already been done, which can lead to months (or even more if we look at Ras Laffan Industrial City, which Qatar stated can take 3 to 5 years to repair over a month ago) of high prices in key commodities.

Also, we’re currently looking at a ceasefire, not a “complete” peace agreement - which remains yet to be seen - and although I argued before why I don’t expect this conflict to last for that long given the military, logistic, political, economic, religious, etc. implications, the timing of a recovery in the region’s commodity flow/exports is an uncertainty that still hangs on the current valuations.

Regardless, the inflationary shock that can come from this can still be significant, as fuel prices remain high alongside vital commodities such as fertilizers, which can add up to significant inflationary pressure in the near term, especially if countries/other buyers rush in to replenish their strategic reserves while they still can, all while the supply is relatively limited.

Although this and the Supreme Court’s tariff decision can still be good for trade, shipping costs and avoiding significant supply chain issues due to shipping delays since companies were reportedly avoiding even the Suez Canal due to the risk, we’re still looking at something that can last for far longer and get far worse than a temporary rise in pump prices, which can lead to a response from the Fed and other central banks that can further slow down the economy.

Ultimately, the previous round of rate hikes was a major part of the weakness reported by most companies, especially in the US and China - which goes well beyond the apparel industry - with significant trade-downs and pressure on discretionary budgets, so it’s no wonder why LULU and others retreated from what were ultimately elevated prices some years ago.

As mentioned before, LULU saw some interest from activist investors and even the company’s founder, who recently kept calling for a return to lululemon’s yoga pants roots, highlighting failures such as the Breezethrough recall or the more recent one for Get Low seen at the beginning of 2026, and arguing that “continued brand deterioration contributed” to the company's massive decline seen over the past couple of years, and the fact that they’re now investigated by the Attorney General of Texas for “forever chemicals” in their activewear surely doesn’t help - regardless of the outcome and merits.

This can add more pressure on the company to improve, with the CEO change announced not long after activist fund Elliott Investment Management announced a $1 billion stake, making it one of LULU’s largest shareholders and obviously influencing the company’s future in such a crucial time.

I’ll mention again that having so much interest from an industry veteran and one of the largest activist investors out there is quite hard to ignore, as it can highlight that we’re still talking about a very attractive base where these investors see significant potential to reshape and develop, with the price making it even more attractive now.

Not only that, but we could even see a global giant buy them given these factors, with major peers like LVMH (LVMUY) being able to integrate the business into their strong global supply chain and accelerate their international expansion, name new management, and benefit from potential synergies in a long-term recovery in consumer demand.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, the company has to navigate a very tough consumer environment, which can be even harder now while the CEO transition is advancing, and even though the right person can turn them around, that also comes with its own risks and potential skepticism from the market until proven otherwise.

At the same time, the Iran conflict can still lead to significant pressure on already weak discretionary budgets regardless of the ceasefire or even a potential peace agreement in the next few weeks, as the damage done to the region’s infrastructure can still hurt the global economy for much longer after the conflict is over.

Valuation

For LULU’s DCF to Equity valuation, given the expected hit from trade changes, their mitigation efforts, inventory wind-downs, and a normalization of working capital, I’ll assume an FCF of $1 billion in 2026 and a recovery to $1.4 billion in 2027, assuming a tougher year now while they (and the activist investor) work on adapting for the “new era” of trade, with no changes in net borrowings since their spotless balance sheet doesn’t need any.

This would grow at a CAGR of 7.5% until 2030 thanks to the potential to see low single-digit growth in the Americas (although a proper recovery can lead to even more) alongside double-digit international expansion, with a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, as well as a 10% discount rate to demand a solid margin of safety given the industry they play in, with 117.2 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

As a result, we get to an equity value of $21.11 billion, for an intrinsic value of $180.11 per share, which is still above the current $168.28 seen at the moment of writing this.

Note, however, that this doesn’t assume the effect of buybacks, which can lead to significantly better prices, with a 4% buyback yield during this time, for example, resulting in a price of $249.18 under the same assumptions, which is well above the current levels.

Conclusions

LULU is a Buy, backed by an attractive valuation that already takes into account a solid margin of safety and solid potential to recover, with a strong foundation that can be modeled into a strong long-term performance.

There are risks to remember given the industry, competition, new trade structure, inflationary pressure, and more, but the company is working on addressing them, while a new CEO and activist involvement can help spin this around.

data.title: Automatic Data Processing: A Compelling Short Opportunity
data.description:
ADP (ADP) has been beaten up YTD, down nearly 21%, with the slide being a continuation of the second half of last year's weakness. AI fears are clearly mounting and despite decent financial results out of the company the last few quarters, the stock is getting very little credit. This prompts a deeper dive to explore whether or not the market has it correct and what the next likely move for ADP is. I believe that ADP is in the process of placing itself in a disadvantageous position and it needs to act quickly before it is materially disrupted by AI.

Structural Concerns Mounting

ADP is one of the largest companies in the human resources and payroll solutions space and perhaps the most well-known. At over $20 billion in revenue in the prior fiscal year, this is a massive organization that is the backbone of payroll processing globally. 2025 was a year of solid growth, too, with revenue up nearly 7% on both a reported and organic basis.

This is not a terribly complex company - they have two divisions: Employer Services and PEO Services. The best way to think about the difference between the two is that while Employer Services does the HR software/payroll processing, PEO services offers benefits, workers comp, risk assessment, and compliance tools.

Given the advent to vibe coding, AI, and LLM and the limited amount of 1P/3P data required to start a competitive business to an HR software/payroll processing business, ADP's stock has come under pressure.

There is now more competition than I can even compile for this space because of AI advancements. Players like Gusto, Rippling, Deel, HiBob as well as HR-unique startups like Shapes, Orbio or TalentHR, everyone is vying for a slice of the pie that ADP has the dominant market share in. Other than having been first to market, there isn't much to be defensive about with this name.

But, it's not enough to say that ADP is susceptible to AI risk. Many software companies are right now. Some will be able to circumvent the risk from new entrants, most likely through acquisition of smaller players for those acquirer's that have great balance sheets, but others will be unable to keep up, especially as price becomes a lever.

So, how at risk is ADP?

ADP is the largest player in the HCM market which has a TAM of $180 billion according to their 2025 Investor Day presentation. That would put their share at just about 11%. The combination of competitors Workday, SAP, Oracle and UKG make up approximately 45-50% of the total market according to KPMG.

Workday (WDAY) has a similar issue to ADP - it's stock is on the severe decline and their CTO Peter Bailis left last month to go be a Member of the Technical Staff of Anthropic. Working at Anthropic is likely a dream for anyone, but this was a C-level executive of a major public company worth $32 billion. What does that tell you about the forward outlook for the HCM industry?

ADP is going to have issues on its hands especially when competitors like Gusto go public. Other competitors like Rippling and Deel have not yet been rumored to go public just yet, but this is something to keep an eye on. While these three are all private companies, their valuation rounds have gained considerable attention. For instance, Gusto secured a $9.5 billion valuation last year with $175 million raised in their latest round mid-last year.

Financial Profile and Balance Sheet Optionality

In revisiting the guide ahead of their upcoming report, there's segment guides to consider.

Employment Services (ES) -

  • Revenue +6%
  • ES New Business Bookings +4-7%
  • Client Revenue Retention -30bps to -10bps
  • US Pays per Control ~flat

PEO Services (PEO) -

  • Revenue +5-7%
  • Revenue ex-ZMPT 3-5% (ZMPT is zero-margin benefits pass-throughs essentially business done on a gross basis rather than net)
  • Average WSEs ~+2% (WSEs are worksite employees - essentially a barometer for unit/client growth in this division)

When we put all this together, we get a very robust guide. Given the level of detail, I am pasting it below from their last slide deck. Aside from the previously listed segment top-line drivers, they're targeting adj. EBIT margin expansion of 50-70bps and adj. diluted EPS growth of 9-10%.

Source: Investor Presentation

ADP's balance sheet is going to be their biggest asset. Payroll processing is not going away any time soon and certainly not in the time frame that investors should consider. The company has effectively at least $5-10 billion to work with to spend on acquisitions and I truly believe that's the next step.

Speculating on M&A is not a great investment strategy unless there is a forced hand. ADP isn't in a great spot - they're not a nimble organization and they're the incumbent that can be undercut by fast growing competitors who are quicker to market and will use price as a lever.

In October they announced they were buying Pequity, but this was for an immaterial sum to the point that it wasn't disclosed and didn't move the needle on guidance. The year prior is when they actually shelled out $1.2 billion to buy WorkForce Software. The issue is that for their scale, this is still a bolt-on acquisition and they need to go big or they risk getting permanently disrupted.

On the last earnings call, Kartik Mehta a sell-side analyst ask the question about AI and the impact it's having on employment for their clients and then how the company thinks about their own headcount. Here was CFO Peter Hadley's response:

"Yeah. Thanks for the question, Kartik. In terms of the headcount, no, we have not sort of taken a different approach to our headcount. We remain committed to growing sales headcount. We have seen over decades the contribution that that can make. What it has done to your point is it has enabled our sellers to be both more efficient and, I think, also more effective. I would still say we are in the relatively early innings in terms of taking dividends, if you like, from these investments and really seeing sort of the lift we expect to get from this over the coming years. But it is less about, okay, you know, a shift change in how we approach investing in the Salesforce or sort of where we expect sales to come from, really, it is a way that we are looking to make our salespeople more effective, more efficient, and ultimately deliver more wins. But I think you should expect us to continue to invest in both headcount and tools, be they AI and also other tools. We have spoken about, you know, the zone, which obviously is AI-infused, but it is also a platform our sellers use. All of those things, we will continue to invest in to maximize our opportunity to be successful on the sales front."

What company on the verge of disruption doubles down on fixed cost, let alone headcount? It makes very little sense to me. This company needs to hold an Investor Day/Analyst Community day and walk through their competitive response to AI.

They held one last year and usually companies wait 3-5 years before hosting another one, but the landscape evolving at a pace they cannot keep up with. There are not many times where companies should lever up and pursue growth, but this is one of them because otherwise they face severe market share losses in this low-barrier to entry industry. In my opinion, management should pursue a major AI acquisition before the valuations become even larger.

My favorite part about this story is that they have a slide from last year that talks about Agentic AI - the best buzzword of 2025. Agentic AI is meant to perform tasks autonomously with minimal human input and drive efficiencies as a result, leading to a margin uplift in most businesses. Instead, here we are 9 months later and the company itself interprets it as a reason to double down on salesforce headcount? There is no well-defined strategy, here.

Source: Investor Presentation

Valuation and Trajectory

Now, if we take this guide at face value and assume that it can be hit, it's important to think about how that impacts forward trajectory.

At the midpoint, they would do approximately $21.8bn in revenue, have an adjusted operating margin of 26.6% and adj. EPS of about $11. That would place the stock today trading at 18.2x. For a MSD% top line business with double-digit earnings growth, that's a very fair multiple and one I could even make a case for having some upside towards low-20s given the relative historical stability and consistency of the business. Street is modeling FY26/FY27 EPS at $11.03/$12.33 so effectively guidance needs to be hit and then do another ~10% next year. Effectively, no margin for error with AI, which is interesting.

ADP, like clockwork, puts up earnings beats. Look below at how clean their track record is. In the last decade, they average a 3 cent nominal beat/2.3% average beat versus street estimates with the median at 1.4%. Small beats and nothing to run home about, but consistent beats no less.

Source: Passage Research

I think the right way to think about the multiple here is that it should trade towards 10-11x EV/EBITDA. Even if guidance is able to be hit and the impact of AI is delayed, the overhang will not cease to exist. FY27 EBITDA estimates are approximately $6.6 billion which with an EV of $82.5 billion, you're looking at about 2.5 turns of multiple compression on the way before it starts to look interesting from a valuation standpoint. The dividend yield will near 4% at that point and the free cash flow yield will be approaching high-single-digits.

Source: Passage Research

At 10x EV/EBITDA, I'm looking for ADP to trade down to $160.

With that being said the dividend yield has just surpassed 3.3%, so it's a clear income candidate that is likely popping up on many investors' screeners. This is complimented by a free cash flow yield of nearly 6%. The company has net debt of $1.5 billion with negligible net leverage (at just about a quarter turn).

Source: Passage Research

Risks

ADP could stop AI as a competitive threat and then the company, given its defensive historical characteristics, could re-rate quickly, to the tune of 5-6 turns in a rapid amount of time and squeeze AI-focused shorts in the process. This would most likely be accomplished via M&A of a major competitor such that they gain AI capabilities that create real switching costs that aren't easily disrupted by new AI tools. I would say a more realistic risk is that they find ways with AI to become structurally more profitable and rip out cost, all the while finding small levers to blunt competitive forces and prove out their historical consistency of operating performance once more. If the financials don't crack, the stock is due to rally.

The stock has been crushed already, which is one of the largest risks to shorting it, here. In a hedge fund/long only de-grossing tape (similar to the one we are experiencing now) or in a momentum reversal tape, ADP will meaningfully outperform, but past that the situation for the stock is pretty dire.

Conclusion

ADP doesn't seem too concerned about the forthcoming threat from AI competitors, especially large private players that could IPO as soon as early next year and force fund flows out of their stock and into competitors. ADP is one of the largest players in the HCM industry but is seemingly the slowest to react. Management needs to get aggressive on the acquisition front or they will be forced to play a pricing war versus new competitors.

data.title: VB: Small Caps Join The Breakout Party
data.description:
Small caps loudly joined the S&P 500 at a fresh all-time high following encouraging peace-talk developments between the United States and Iran. On Friday, April 17, the Vanguard Small-Cap ETF (VB) reached a new record, finally eclipsing its February 20, 2026, peak. Zoom out, and we find that VB trails only international equities among the S&P 500, mid-caps, and the Vanguard FTSE All-World ex-US ETF (VEU).

I was bullish on VB back in November 2025, and shares have returned 15% since then, dividends included. That’s 9 percentage points of alpha to the S&P 500. Today, despite a torrid rally off the March low, I reiterate a "Buy" rating. Amid increasing earnings estimates and the temperature coming down on the geopolitical front, I expect this week’s breakout to be durable.

Small Caps Leading Among U.S. Sizes YoY

Small Caps: Higher Yield, Bigger Buyback Percentage Compared To S&P 500

Small-Cap EPS Forecasts Rising

According to the issuer, VB seeks to track the performance of the CRSP US Small Cap Index, which measures the investment return of small-capitalization stocks and offers investors a convenient way to match the performance of a diversified group of small companies. The ETF employs a passively managed, full-replication approach.

VB is a large ETF, with more than $70 billion in assets under management as of April 16, 2026. Its annual expense ratio is low at only 3 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is about on par with that of the S&P 500 at 1.25%.

Share-price momentum is strong right now, earning VB a solid B+ ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. In terms of risk, VB scores well, thanks to its somewhat modest standard deviation percentage (the conflict in the Middle East, notwithstanding) and in-check annual volatility level. At the moment, implied volatility has dipped to 19%, which is the lowest since late February.

On liquidity, VB also scores well. Average daily volume is north of 800,000 shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at 3 basis points, per Vanguard.

For an update on the portfolio, the 4-star, Gold-rated ETF by Morningstar has more exposure across the style box than prospective investors may realize. Indeed, more than one-third of the allocation is classified as mid-cap, which is key for factor and style managers to consider. The breakout among value, blend, and growth is about even, which is not too much changed since my previous assessment.

On valuation, VB remains a bargain, in my view. The current price-to-earnings ratio is a bit more than 16x, while the long-term EPS growth rate is formidable at 11.36%. The resulting PEG ratio is attractive at under 1.5.x

VB: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming into the sector breakout, Industrials is the largest component, but it’s only 21% of the product. To go along with that cyclical segment, the growth-centered Information Technology sector is runner-up at 14.2%. Elsewhere, value and defensives enter the scene just before I.T., with Financials and Healthcare rounding out the top 4.

I’d note that among the 11 S&P 500 sector ETFs, only Information Technology (XLK) and Real Estate (XLRE) are notching 52-week highs.

VB: Holdings & Dividend Information

Turning to the calendar, VB has historically performed very well from April through July. So, if the past 10 years of price data holds, the rally that began in late March could have legs well into the third quarter.

VB: Bullish Calendar Trends Through July

The Technical Take

With a compelling valuation, diversified portfolio, and bullish seasonals, VB’s technical situation is very strong. Notice in the chart below that the small-cap ETF has launched to a new all-time high, joining the S&P 500 at a record level. The RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph is likewise tagging its best mark going back to November of 2024. Couple that with a rising long-term 200-day moving average, and it’s clear that the bulls control the primary trend.

Also take a look at the volume by price profile on the left side of the graph. There’s actually a surprisingly high number of shares traded just below the February peak. That tells me that we could see a solid support zone if we see a pullback (although a false breakout would not be an encouraging feature).

In the here and now, I calculate a measured move upside price target to be about $305, based on the depth of the February to March decline and subsequent breakout today. On the downside, a gap lingers at $265, and there’s now a smaller gap just under $280 from the up-move on the day of this writing. While gaps often get filled, not all do. A case in point was one from just about a year ago, after Liberation Day.

VB: Breakout Above the February High, Rising 200dma, Strong RSI Momentum Confirmation

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on VB. The small-cap index ETF appears to be a solid value, given earnings growth trends and the current P/E. Technical price action is very strong, especially with today’s breakout and sanguine seasonal trends.

data.title: Low-Stress 8% Yields I Would Bet My Retirement On
data.description:
While there are plenty of high-yielding stocks out there, there are very few that can be counted on through thick and thin to continue paying at their current level. Therefore, it can be a difficult environment for retirees who want to significantly juice their retirement income without going far out on the risk spectrum.

In this article, I'm going to detail two exceptions to this rule: one which is an equity that yields 8.2% on the next 12-month basis and is expected to continue growing its distribution at an inflation-beating pace for the foreseeable future, and the other one is a broadly diversified ETF that pays out monthly dividends and yields about 8% on an annualized basis.

An Actively Managed Bond ETF Worth Owning

The first opportunity to discuss is an actively managed bond fund called the Infrastructure Capital Bond ETF (BNDS). BNDS implements an actively managed high-conviction bond strategy while giving investors a simple, liquid ETF that they can use to access it easily. Rather than tracking a cap-weighted index of heavily covered issuers like many of its peers, such as the SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK) and the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG), BNDS instead scours the fixed income space for mispriced credits, with a particular focus on real asset-intensive sectors like energy pipelines, mortgage rates, and real estate businesses.

For example, they purchased Plains All American Pipeline (PAA)(PAGP) bonds at an 8.5% yield, which was an incredible bargain for them. Given that PAA has a solid balance sheet, strong tangible asset collateral, and long-duration fee-based contracts, that yield was highly sustainable, and the bond investment had to have a very low risk of loss over the long term. BNDS looks for other opportunities like this that it can successfully arbitrage, and then regularly recycles capital to help boost its income per share over time while also delivering total return outperformance.

Overall, BNDS has 76 positions as of the end of March, with its top ten holdings making up about 40% of the portfolio. This helps it balance high-conviction positions to generate outperformance potential while still being diversified to make it a low-risk aggregate investment for investors, putting it in a similar category to high-yield bonds in terms of risk while delivering even better income and total return potential.

Growing Dividends From A Bond Fund

The ETF not only pays out an attractive dividend yield of around 8% annualized, but it has actually grown its dividend slightly since its inception back in 2025. This reflects its successful ability to recycle out of positions that appreciate in value and into undervalued positions, as normally bond funds do not generate any dividend growth in and of themselves since their underlying holdings are fixed income in nature. BNDS also does not use any leverage, which keeps its volatility low and puts it on a very sustainable footing even in uncertain market environments.

While its 0.81% expense ratio is fairly high compared to some of its peers, it has outperformed its peers substantially since it launched, therefore fully justifying its higher expense ratio that is used for implementing its active management strategy.

Risks To Keep In Mind

While I think that the fund should be able to pay sustainable and potentially even slightly growing dividends over time, making it an attractive and fairly low-stress income vehicle for retirement, it is still worth noting that BNDS invests primarily in below-investment-grade bonds. While it is diversified, there's still a very real risk of loss should the economy enter a severe economic downturn and/or if the management team makes a poor decision in terms of their bond selection.

Overall, though, the focus on real asset sectors, with about 40% concentration in energy and real estate, helps to mitigate risks, as does the active management, which helps to spot problematic securities early and mitigate those risks to help support the long-term capital preservation and income generation goals of the fund. It is also worth knowing that rising long-term interest rates would also push the NAV lower, even if the underlying credits remain sound.

An 8.2%-Yielder With Serious Growth Potential

The other high-yield opportunity that I like a lot right now is a midstream MLP (AMLP) that issues a K-1 by the name of MPLX LP (MPLX). MPLX is majority owned by Marathon Petroleum (MPC) and operates in two core segments:

  • The crude and refined products and logistics segment, which makes up about 65% of its EBITDA

  • Its natural gas and energy services segment, which makes up about 35% of its EBITDA

MPLX is largely commodity-price resistant as it generates most of its cash flows in this segment from fixed-rate contracts, minimum volume commitments, and inflation-linked escalators, which enabled it to support a very high and growing distribution. Additionally, it is leaning heavily into its natural gas and energy services segment, as that is where significant growth potential is available, as evidenced by its 90% of growth capex this year being allocated toward that segment.

Impressive Pipeline Of Growth Projects

Growth projects coming online in the next few years include:

  • The Secretariat 2 processing plant in the Permian Basin

  • The Titan sour gas treatment complex

  • The BANGL pipeline expansion

  • Harmon Creek 3

  • The LPG export terminal joint venture, which is expected to come online in 2028

Each of these projects has been underwritten at a mid-teens unlevered return and should help the company to deliver its mid-to-high single-digit EBITDA growth rate over the next few years and potentially beyond. MPLX also has a strong balance sheet with a BBB credit rating from S&P, over $2 billion in cash on hand, a distribution coverage ratio of above 1.3 times, and a leverage ratio that is well within its target range, especially once accounting for its numerous growth projects coming online in the near future.

MPLX's 8.2% next 12-month yield, when combined with management guiding for 12.5% annualized distribution growth for at least the next year or two and analyst consensus estimates that have the distribution growing at a 10.6% CAGR through 2028, means that it is one of the most attractive combined current income and distribution growth opportunities in the market today.

Why Risks Are Manageable

Of course, no investment is risk-free, and MPLX is no exception, as it is largely beholden to MPC since it generates a lot of its cash flow from MPC as a counterpart, and MPC is a major unit holder of MPLX. That being said, it is a symbiotic relationship, as MPC generates a lot of cash flow from MPLX, and thus far, MPLX's phenomenal track record indicates MPC is running it fairly well.

Additionally, MPLX is leaning aggressively into growth projects, which, if they fail to deliver the return management expects, could result in materially slower distribution growth for the MLP moving forward. Of course, operating in the energy sector can also see volatility in its unit price with energy price fluctuations, though its underlying cash flows tend to be much more resilient overall.

The Retirement Portfolio Combo That Could Let You Sleep at Night

I think these two investment opportunities can combine well in a retirement portfolio because BNDS provides fixed income every month from a diversified portfolio of bonds, while MPLX is an equity play based on essential infrastructure that also has inflation resistance built into it. Combined, they deliver income north of 8% that should grow at a rate that, in aggregate, grows roughly at the rate of inflation — slightly even faster depending on weightings between these two positions, making them great additions to a low-stress income-focused retirement portfolio that investors can bet on while sleeping well at night. This is why I hold both of them in my 7–8% yielding total return outperforming and below-market-beta retirement portfolio at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cintas: The Valuation Has Come In, But It's Not A Buy Just Yet
data.description:
Introduction

Since my initial piece on Cintas Corporation (CTAS) back in September 2024, shares of Cintas have fallen 11% while the S&P 500 has gone on to move up 27%. At the time, I recommended that new investors wait for a more attractive entry point as the valuation on Cintas looked pricey. That said, I think Cintas is a name worth revisiting as shares have lagged and the valuation multiple has compressed since the company is a high quality business with reoccurring revenues and steady margin expansion. This article will be an update to my original investment thesis where I’ll unpack what’s changed, share my expectations for Cintas in 2026, and discuss how I’m thinking about the valuation at these levels.

A look back at the recent quarter

Cintas’ core business looks healthy. In many ways, Q3’26 was pretty uneventful as the company put up consistent numbers that were largely in line with what the market had expected. On revenues, Cintas achieved a record for the quarter at $2.84 billion, which was up 8.9% compared to last year and 8.2% organically after adjusting for acquisitions.

As illustrated in the chart below, each route-based business contributed meaningfully, with First Aid and Safety Services posting 14.6% organic growth, Fire Protection Services at 10.0%, Uniform Rental and Facility Services at 7.3%, and Uniform Direct Sales at 3.1%. With strong performance across all of its segments, Cintas has been able to gain new customers, grow with existing customers, and maintaining pricing that’s been consistent with their historical 2-3% range. So in an environment where investors are potentially worried by AI disruption, conflict in the Middle East, among other concerns, Cintas is one of those steady names that has diversified exposure to resilient verticals in health care, hospitality, education, and government.

Down the income statement, Cintas’ profitability has continued to improve with gross margins expanding 40bps to 51.0% of sales. By segment, gross margins have improved across all three segments with margins of 50.3% in Uniform Rental and Facility Services, 58.1% in First Aid and Safety Services, and 50.5% in Fire Protection Services. A lot of this expansion has come from a combination of cost management and supply chain efficiencies. Further down, operating income rose 8.2% to $659.9 million, good for a 23.2% margin (up 40 basis points adjusted). With SG&A flat as a percentage of revenue, the company’s net income was $502.5 million and EPS of $1.24 was right in line with sellside estimates.

Leverage is manageable even after UniFirst acquisition

As for the balance sheet, Cintas maintains a solid credit profile with A rated debt from S&P with a stable outlook (source: Bloomberg). With debt of $2.6 billion against LTM EBITDA of $3.1 billion, leverage is less than one turn. As such, the company can access debt capital markets at low rates as evidenced by the weighted average coupon on existing debt at 4.12% (source: Bloomberg).

However, subsequent to the quarter, Cintas made a deal to acquire its competitor, UniFirst in a $5.5 billion transaction that consists of cash and stock. Paying a massive premium (over 60%) above where UniFirst was trading during its 2025 lows, Cintas had clearly had its eye on the business in the past as they tried to make a bid (unsuccessful) back in 2022.

On the balance sheet side, leverage is expected to remain modest at approximately 1.5x Debt/EBITDA following the anticipated closing of the UniFirst acquisition in 2H’26. Because of the deal, I think buybacks might be put on hold in the short term but this is likely a great deal longer term given the cost savings that the combined business will generate.

Cintas is set up well in 2026 post-UniFirst deal

Cintas’ management came out of the Q3 call optimistic and raised guidance. The new forecast projects revenue of $11.21-$11.24 billion (8.4%-8.7% total growth) and EPS of $4.86-$4.90 (10.5%-11.4% growth), excluding $0.03-$0.04 in UniFirst transaction costs.

I find this guidance reasonable and not overly optimistic, given Cintas' history of outperforming amid macro headwinds like elevated fuel costs (1.7% of revenue, with a 30% fuel spike adding just 30bps impact) and complex economic conditions. On the conference call, management noted that they’ve been consistent on pricing 2-3% higher annually and maintain high retention in the 95% range with new business/cross-sell fueling mid-to-high single-digit growth.

In terms of the UniFirst acquisition, my first impression was that this was a smart, disciplined deal that catapults Cintas to market leadership. Valued at $5.5 billion enterprise value ($310/share: $155 cash + 0.772 Cintas shares at $200.77), it implies around an 8x TTM EBITDA on their business pre-synergies. As it’s being financed via cash/credit/bridge for pro forma leverage at 1.5x, Cintas isn’t overleveraging itself to do this. Perhaps prudently and conservatively, they’re preserving buybacks/dividends post-close.

Management hosted an M&A call where they highlighted how both businesses have complementary footprints. Cintas is adding 300k new customers with the expected total reaching 1.5 million. Importantly, no revenue synergies are being modeled and instead the company is focused on $375 million in cost savings over four years from procurement, tech integration, plant efficiencies, and G&A cuts.

This seems a lot like the G&K playbook from 2017. Back then, Cintas was buying a business at a $2.2 billion enterprise value and was expecting to generate synergies of $130-$140 million in annual synergies. At a single-digit EBITDA multiple, the company’s focus on seamless integration and cutting costs made this a pretty successful deal. I see a lot of similarities between that deal and UniFirst.

Unlike the G&K deal that took four full years to achieve synergies, Cintas expects UniFirst synergies to be much faster with EPS accretion by the end of year two post-close. While 8x EBITDA isn’t cheap, it’s justified by UniFirst's quality (long-term oriented, tech catch-up underway) and Cintas' proven M&A execution. My expectation is that this should probably help boost margins 100-200 bps long-term. Even though they aren’t baking it into their expectations, there could be some cross-sell like Cintas' safety/fire to UniFirst uniforms and that could help on the revenue side. In total, the deal adds growth, enhances route density, and helps position the business well against e-commerce/hybrid threats.

As for what to watch next quarter in Q4’26, I’d expect more formal guidance for FY’27. Given that Cintas has beaten sellside estimates in all of the last eight quarters, the company is likely to beat again since Q4 EPS estimates are only a penny higher than Q3 (source: Bloomberg). On the revenue side, sales should probably be up 8% with continued organic growth of 7-8% from pricing, retention (95%+), and new wins in uniforms/facility services, plus strength in high-margin first aid (14%+ organic) and fire protection (10%). But we aren’t likely to see the benefits of UniFirst until we get to the back half of the fiscal year.

Risks

In terms of the risks, while Cintas' business model is resilient, the risk I see in the short-term is on regulatory hurdles the company might face for the UniFirst acquisition amid antitrust scrutiny from the FTC, potentially delaying close (H2 2026) or requiring divestitures. There’s a $350 million reverse termination fee on the deal where Cintas would have to pay UniFirst $350 million if regulators block the deal on antitrust grounds so that could be a potential cash outflow without a benefit to the company if the deal doesn’t close.

If and when the deal closes, there are always integration challenges post-merger. Unlike G&K or the smaller tuck-in deals that Cintas does, this is a fairly substantial deal and so there might be more tech/ERP harmonization that’s required or higher customer churn that we might see. Finally, macro and cost pressures like fuel and labor inflation could impact margins if not fully passed on through pricing.

Valuation still pricey

While I’m a fan of Cintas’ business and the deal they just announced, what holds me back is on the valuation. Even though it’s re-rated from the 27x EV/EBITDA range to 22.0x EV/EBITDA today, I think this is still a high multiple to pay, despite recognizing the quality of the earnings.

On current numbers, Cintas is trading at roughly double the valuation that they paid for UniFirst and trades at 33.4x P/E, much higher than peers at 24.6x (source: Bloomberg). Even when factoring in mid-teens EPS growth from the sellside for the next few years up to $7.57 in EPS by 2030, this is still a business that’s trading at 23.4x P/E by the end of the decade.

Conclusion

In my view, not much has changed about the core of the story other than the recent deal. Cintas Corporation remains a high-quality compounder with durable growth, strong margins, and a proven ability to execute both organically and inorganically. As illustrated, the UniFirst deal will likely add a compelling long-term layer to that thesis, and I think management has earned it given their track record. That said, I’m not convinced the current valuation gives investors enough margin for error in the near term and so even after the pullback the stock still trades at an above market multiple that assumes a lot goes right. I’d prefer a wider margin of safety before stepping in. For now, I’m staying patient and maintaining a ‘hold’ rating. This is a name I want to own, just not at this price.

data.title: The Final Push Toward A Deal And Why I'm Now Fully Invested
data.description:
Happy Friday, everyone!

I hope it's a green day in your portfolio as well. As we've seen in the past few days, markets do not wait for certainty, and the past few days in the Middle East have made that painfully clear (for the bears, of course).

It is easy to get headline fatigue (I am almost burned out, to be honest), but I think we are in the last sprint before we can take a break from checking major media outlets every now and then.

Markets have quickly shifted the tone from fear to optimism after the U.S.-brokered ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon, Iran’s decision to keep the Strait of Hormuz open to commercial traffic (with caveats), and reports of progress toward a broader U.S.-Iran nuclear understanding.

As I'm writing, I'm seeing pretty much every sector in the green, except energy and utilities.

In this piece, I cover what to watch for in what I believe is the final sprint of the Middle East conflict. I will also provide a rundown of how I shifted from neutral to bullish as soon as April 9.

On the risk side, I admit there is a lot of optimism grounded in one single pillar: a deal that puts an end to the war and reopens the Strait of Hormuz. If the second round of talks fails, I believe the rally we've seen over the last few days will quickly revert.

My view is that there is light at the end of the tunnel. Given the recent development, I depleted my 19% cash position, and I'm heading into the weekend talks fully invested.

A Quick Recap Of The Developments in The Middle East

There are three main headlines that caught my eye these last few days.

The first one is the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon, brokered by the U.S. and agreed on April 16.

There are three main points that I wanted to highlight from the ceasefire, as they are quite significant for Lebanon.

As for Israel, it seems that its military remains as much as 10 km inside southern Lebanon as part of a buffer zone to prevent further attacks. In other words, this seems like another fragile ceasefire, largely propped up by the U.S., as noted by Trump's post:

Israel will not be bombing Lebanon any longer. They are PROHIBITED from doing so by the U.S.A. Enough is enough!!!

The second headline (related to the one above) that caught the market's attention was the announcement by Iran’s foreign minister on 17 April that the Strait of Hormuz is open for all commercial vessels for the remaining period of the 10-day Lebanon ceasefire.

Interestingly, President Trump went further and said in a post that Iran had agreed never to close Hormuz again.

Now, the details matter. According to a Reuters report, a senior Iranian official said that all ships can sail through, including U.S. commercial vessels, but passage must be coordinated with the IRGC and Iran’s Ports and Maritime Organization.

Notably, transit appears to remain limited to lanes Iran deems safe, and military vessels remain prohibited from transiting.

How about the U.S. blockade? Well, it appears that the U.S. naval blockade remains active for traffic to and from Iranian ports until the U.S.-Iran deal is complete.

This brings me to the third headline:

According to Bloomberg, Trump said in a phone interview that Iran agreed to an indefinite suspension of its nuclear program and would receive no frozen U.S. funds. On top of that, he noted that talks over a lasting deal will "probably" be held over the weekend, noting "most of the main points are finalized. It’ll go pretty quickly."

As I'm writing this piece, Iran hasn't confirmed this concession.

However, the markets have spoken:

As I'm writing, I have a 0.4% cash position in my portfolio. A few days back, I had a 19% cash position that I decided to keep on the sidelines during the weekend (just in case the talks fail for the second time). Not anymore. Below, I explain why (and how) I turned bullish in the broader markets.

Why (And How) I Turned Bullish

At first, I wasn't sold on the idea of a ceasefire.

Back on March 20, I published a piece titled: "Even With Hormuz Reopened, I Still See The S&P 500 At 6,100."

Here is the rationale from that article:

The catalyst?

Inflation is likely to remain high between March and June (at least), as the oil flows will take a few months to normalize after the conflict eventually comes to an end in the Middle East. Therefore, the post-war relief rally shouldn't last more than a few weeks.

Back then, I was overly concerned that price hikes would spill over beyond transportation services or airfares. I mean, things you buy in a supermarket don't just happen to appear on the shelves, right?

Therefore, if fuel (particularly diesel) prices spiked, why would corporations decide not to pass through the additional fuel costs?

Add on top of fuel inflation, shortages in fertilizer (urea, ammonia, and broader nitrogen), both due to the Hormuz blockade and Ukrainian attacks on Russian plants, and the outlook doesn't seem to be particularly exciting for risk assets.

Back in late March, the icing on the cake was the skepticism I noted from both "expert" economists and prediction markets about rate cuts. Even today, the CME Group survey data is showing the most likely date for a rate cut as June 2027.

Therefore, I didn't fully buy the March core CPI forecast from Cleveland's Fed inflation nowcasting tool. For reference, here is another excerpt from my late March 2026 article that shows the precise forecast back then:

The latest update from the Cleveland Fed nowcast shows headline CPI at 3.02% y/y, core CPI at 2.60% y/y, headline PCE at 3.14% y/y, and core PCE at 2.97% y/y for March:

I thought that was too conservative, as the core CPI for March (which excludes energy and food prices) was projected at only 2.60%. For reference, the core CPI in February was 2.5%.

What happens with apparel, recreation, and household furnishings prices (I was wondering in my mind)? In hindsight, my skepticism of the inflation nowcast turned out to be a mistake.

Fast forward a few weeks, and the BEA released the CPI report for March. It turns out that core CPI came in at exactly 2.6%. That's why I wrote a piece on April 10, titled: "March CPI Just Flipped The Inflation Debate."

Why?

With inflation fears mostly out of the way, I believe the market is now underestimating the number of rate cuts this year (remember the table above from the CME Group). As I discussed multiple times, I think Kevin Warsh will cut interest rates this year, despite the current skepticism. Here is the rationale from my April 10 article:

Therefore, if the April/May CPI shows a similar result, likely incoming Fed Chair Kevin Warsh will have one more reason to (aggressively) cut interest rates, on top of the one below:

That said, my first strong buy rating after the start of the conflict in the Middle East came in one day earlier, on April 9, from an article titled: "This Ceasefire Rally Could Collapse Tomorrow - Here's What I'm Buying Anyway."

If you read that piece, you may notice how skeptical I was about the fragile ceasefire. However, I decided to look beyond the near-term pressures and deploy part of the capital that I've been raising since late January this year.

I'm glad I did, because a few days later came the news that the U.S. had blockaded vessels bound for Iran, and that was when I connected the dots in my article, "Why the U.S. Blockade May Be the Move That Ends the War."

Looking Ahead

We're not out of the woods yet.

As I'm writing, Iran hasn't confirmed the nuclear concessions, and the transits through the Strait of Hormuz are still in coordination with the IRGC using lanes that Iran deems as safe. Therefore, I cannot help but wonder whether the toll booth between Larak Island and Qeshm Island is still in effect.

As for normal passage through the strait, there is still a lot of uncertainty due to possible mines along the route that vessels used before the conflict began. If mines were actually laid, clearing that area will take some time.

In regards to the number of transits, there were 8 ships on the first full day of the U.S. blockade, including three Iran-linked tankers. In other words, the traffic was barely affected.

The big question over the weekend, or until the April 22 deadline, is whether there will be a final deal between the U.S. and Iran. Given the recent developments, I think there's a good chance the answer is yes. At the very least, I expect the extension of a ceasefire and some concessions from both sides regarding Hormuz transits (including the U.S. blockade of Iran-bound vessels).

The risk is the fragility of the talks. If the second round of talks ends without a deal, and the conflict resumes on April 22, I believe the rally will quickly revert. I am willing to take this risk, but I'm mindful that the chance of this draconian scenario is not zero.

data.title: CGCV: Likely To Underperform IVV Owing Low Beta Names That Restrain Upside Capture
data.description:
I believe that the Capital Group Conservative Equity ETF (CGCV) will underperform the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) this year, as the low-beta ingredient that bolstered its returns in January–February will likely become a detractor. To be clear, I expect the S&P 500 to reach 7,550 by the end of the year, which implies an around 6% gain from its level as of writing this article. It is unlikely that CGCV will keep pace with it.

Given the Strait of Hormuz situation that was the main contributor to expectations for higher inflation and potentially even higher interest rates is resolved, I anticipate equities sporting strong growth (and especially those having robust GARP metrics) characteristics to outperform those in the value and low volatility camps. CGCV is underweight in growth, has unappealing GARP metrics, and is overweight in low volatility and value, which will restrain its upside capture, and there is historical data supporting that point. I do not see a long-term alpha potential either, again owing to its tilt towards less volatile names that are inherently incapable of capturing sufficient market upside during bull markets.

With that being said, as I have already explained in the August 2025 article, CGCV has a sensible strategy and it does deliver on its three objectives of "current income, growth of capital and conservation of principal," which secures a Hold rating. Also, I still believe that the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV) is a stronger option both factor- and performance-wise, and I will explain why below in the note.

CGCV Strategy And Portfolio

As we know from the Q4 fact sheet available on the Capital Group website, the actively managed CGCV

...strives for the balanced accomplishment of three objectives: current income, growth of capital and conservation of principal.

It is also explained that CGCV is "conservatively managed to reduce volatility and risk," so it "...has the potential to provide downside resilience during market declines." And as a mild spoiler, I should mention that it does deliver on these fronts but with nuances.

CGCV Portfolio Discussion

I believe that at this juncture, the CGCV portfolio is offering mostly robust factor exposures, mainly in terms of value and quality (there is room for improvement though), which could secure a downside capture ratio of around 80% if the market narrative once again switches towards a more pessimistic one, for instance, if investors start to price in an new round of escalation or question the AI growth premia, etc.

However, my concern here is that a sufficient allocation to growth (and especially GARP) names is clearly lacking, which, together with its tilt towards less volatile names, will likely restrain its upside capture this year and quite likely beyond, translating into underperformance versus IVV.

More specifically, as of April 10, there were 71 equities in the CGCV portfolio accounting for 96.6% of the net assets. Another asset class represented was cash & cash equivalents. Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) was the key holding with an around 4.99% weight, followed by Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) that accounted for 4.22%. Importantly, my analysis shows that nearly all CGCV's holdings are S&P 500 constituents (90.5% of the net assets). There are only five exceptions, key among them being Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM).

Now, let us delve deeper into the factor story.

Value and Growth

While CGCV does not ignore the trillion-dollar league completely, it is much lighter in these bellwethers compared to both CGDV and IVV (which tracks its primary benchmark), which is reflected in its weighted average market cap of around $640 billion.

In part as a consequence of that, we see much more robust value characteristics even compared to CGDV (which is, as a reminder, a "dividend value ETF"), including a higher adjusted weighted average earnings yield, a lower Price/Sales ratio (no exposure to NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) helped a lot), etc.

But as I have multiple times explained in my previous articles discussing ETFs, large exposure to the value factor requires compromises, chiefly in terms of growth. And this is exactly the case with CGCV, as its growth metrics are considerably weaker compared to the peers I selected, especially its WA forward EPS growth rate. As a consequence, it is also much lighter in GARP names, as illustrated by the Price/Earnings-to-Growth, the Price/Sales-to-Growth, and the EV/EBITDA-to-Growth ratios.

Quality

I would not say that something is fundamentally wrong with CGCV's quality characteristics, as they are generally fine, but I still believe that it is somewhat underdelivering on that front. For a portfolio that is dominated by mega-caps and has no footprint in mid-caps, an 83.5% allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Profitability grade or higher is too small, especially compared to IVV and CGDV. Its weighted average capital efficiency metrics like the adjusted ROE, ROA, and ROTC also look much weaker.

Low volatility

CGCV maintains a considerable footprint in the information technology sector, precisely like IVV and CGDV. But what makes it considerably different is a much larger allocation to defensive sectors like consumer staples and utilities (18.4% combined weight compared to IVV's 7.4% and 7.37% of CGDV).

In part as a consequence of that (nearly all its holdings from these sectors have 24-month beta coefficients below 0.6, with the only exception being Constellation Energy Corporation (CEG)), the WA beta coefficients of its portfolio are significantly below those of IVV and CGDV, as per my calculations.

Metrics CGCV CGDV IVV
24M Beta 0.78 1.07 1.12
60M Beta 0.84 1.07 1.14

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

And while this fact have both positive (i.e., it secures a less deep maximum drawdown in a correction) and negative effects, I am gravitating toward the view that it will almost certainly restrain CGCV's ability to mirror IVV's gains this year. For clarity, I still anticipate the S&P 500 to touch 7,550 by the end of the year.

CGCV Performance

CGCV offers mostly solid historical performance, as it has gained more than 28% since its inception in June 2024. However, as usual, it is all about nuances.

For example, the low volatility and value factors most likely secured its outperformance versus IVV in January–February (0.5% and 3.42% stronger total returns, respectively). However, I believe that these exact factors were likely the culprits for its underperformance in 2024 and 2025. So since its inception, even though it had a moment earlier this year, CGCV has underperformed the S&P 500 ETF, let alone CGDV.

At the same time, CGCV, despite its conservative strategy, underperformed IVV by 1.14% in March, as it was surprisingly less prepared to tackle the ripple effects from the Iran war.

Next, looking at the data for July 2024–March 2026, we see that while CGCV outperformed IVV during the period, delivering a 0.41% active return, it had an upside capture of only 90.77% compared to CGDV's 121.19%.

So the corollary here is that unless the bearish mood settles over the market again, which I do not anticipate, CGCV will not be able to keep pace with IVV this year and more likely beyond.

Final Thoughts

To conclude, I anticipate the Capital Group Conservative Equity ETF to underperform IVV this year (i.e., deliver less than 6% gain from the current levels), which is the main rationale behind the Hold rating.

Nevertheless, CGCV still could be an interesting option for investors seeking to reduce volatility a little without sacrificing too much of their exposure to the trillion-dollar bellwethers and remain mostly within the S&P 500 universe. CGCV also scores nicely against the liquidity metrics, and its expense ratio is adequate at 33 bps.

However, it is worth keeping in mind that its considerable allocation to low beta defensive names, together with small exposure to growth, can restrain its upside capture, resulting in underperformance versus IVV over the medium- to longer-term.

In this regard, I believe investors seeking higher growth rates and stronger quality and the potential for outperformance this year and more likely beyond should take a closer look at CGDV instead, especially considering its robust performance. All in all, I maintain the Hold rating on CGCV.
